Facts.text: The oldest known Roman temple to Venus is founded, initiating the institution of Vinalia Rustica (grape harvest festival)
St Sixtus III ends his reign as Catholic Pope
The city of Riga is founded
First historical record of Scottish scholar Michael Scot, signs and dates his translation of al-Bitruji's "On the Sphere" in Toledo, Spain
Pope Nicolas IV publishes decree "Supra montem"
A Portuguese ship drifts ashore in the ancient Japanese province of Higo (modern-day Kumamoto Prefecture) (Traditional Japanese date: July 27, 1541)
Saul Wahl is elected King of Poland (according to legend)
Governor of Roanoke Island colony John White returns from England to find no trace of the colonists he left there three years earlier
Spanish army under of general Spinola conquers Lingen
Pendle Witch Trial begins with 10 people accused of witchcraft in Lancaster, England; the key witness is a nine-year-old boy
Urbain Grandier, accused and convicted of sorcery, is burned alive in Loudun, France
The Covenant of the Town of Dedham, Massachusetts, is signed
Giovanni Cassini reports seeing a satellite orbiting Venus
Denmark-Norway, Holstein-Gottorp, and the Swedish Empire sign the Treaty of Travendal
Swedish army lands in Zealand, Denmark
Evening Post begins publishing in Boston, Massachusetts
First public admission to the Salon de Paris art exhibition at the Louvre in Paris
Second Sea Battle of Lagos: England vs. France; ends the following day
A lightning strike on the Bastion of San Nazaro in Brescia, Italy, ignites 90 tons of gunpowder, killing 3,000 people
Curaçao Governor De Veer sends militia to stop rebellious enslaved people
60-70 ft sea serpent sightings are reported offshore in Gloucester, Massachusetts
Scottish explorer Alexander Gordon Laing travels across the Sahara and becomes the first European to reach the fabled trading city of Timbuktu; he is murdered near there a few weeks later
Last Pottawatomie Indians leave Chicago
American Society of Dental Surgeons is founded in New York
French colony established in Akaroa, South Island of New Zealand
General Stephen W. Kearny's US forces capture Santa Fe, New Mexico
Camila O'Gorman and Ladislao Gutiérrez are executed on the orders of Argentine dictator Juan Manuel de Rosas
Netherlands and Japan sign a trade agreement
Sioux Indians begin an uprising in Minnesota; it is later crushed
Battle of Petersburg: Battle of Weldon Railroad, day 1 of a 3-day battle
Sixth day of battle at Deep Bottom Run, Virginia: Confederate assault
French astronomer Pierre Janssen discovers helium in the solar spectrum during an eclipse
Battle of Gravelotte Privat: Prussia defeats France with 32,000 casualties
Aaron Montgomery Ward issues the first catalog for his mail-order business; it is one sheet listing 163 available items
First ascent of Mount Whitney, California (14,494 ft; 4,421 m)
Carr Baker Neel and Samuel Neel win the US Lawn Tennis Association doubles
Hurricane hits Martinique, killing about 700 people
Adolph Ochs (39) buys The New York Times for $75,000
Belgian army withdraws to Antwerp
French troops under General Dubail occupy Sarrebourg
Swiss track and road bicycle racer Oscar Egg sets a new hour world record of 44.247 km at the outdoor Vélodrome Buffalo in Paris; the record stands until 1933
Braves Field opens in Boston to see Braves beat St. Louis Cardinals, 3-1
The Great Fire in Thessaloniki, Greece, destroys 32% of the city, leaving 70,000 individuals homeless
The Royal Netherlands Naval Air Service (MLD) forms
Anti-Cigarette League of America forms in Chicago, Illinois
First-class cricket debut of Walter Hammond
France begins withdrawing troops from the Ruhr
Belgium and US sign treaty about war debts
Cardinal Mercier warns Belgians against socialism and liberalism
First televised weather map is broadcast by the National Oceanic and Atmospheric Administration in Washington, DC
Eastern Airlines begins passenger services
Auguste Piccard and Max Cosyns reach 16,201 meters in a balloon launched from Dübendorf, Switzerland
Englishman James Mollison is the first to fly east to west over the Atlantic
First FM radio construction permit is issued to W1XOJ (WGTR) in Boston, MA
German concentration camp Amersfoort opens
Phillies commit eight errors in a baseball game
Carlson's Raiders land on Makin in the Gilbert Islands and kill 350 Japanese
Otto Skorzeny's Heinkel-111 shot down at Sardinia
Paris rail workers strike against Nazi occupiers
US 15th Army Corps reaches Mantes-Gassicourt near Paris
Scheduled demonstrations at Polo Grounds and Ebbets Field to end segregation in organized baseball are called off
Golf Writers Association of America forms
Naval torpedo and mine factory explodes in Cadiz, Spain, killing 300
Hungary adopts a constitution
Ralph Flanagan and his Orchestra record "You're Breaking My Heart"
Julien Lahaut, the chairman of the Communist Party of Belgium, is assassinated by far-right elements
James E. Wilkins is the first Black person to attend a US cabinet meeting
46.1 cm rainfall in Westfield, Massachusetts (state record)
Hurricane Diane kills 400 people in the US
Shukri al-Quwatli re-elected president of Syria
Cincinnati Reds (8) and Cubs (2) combine to hit 10 home runs in a nine-inning game
Amelia Wershoven sets a world record throwing a baseball 252 feet 4½ inches (76.92 meters)
The US performs a nuclear test at the Nevada Test Site
Actress Betsy Palmer joins Today Show panel
Pérez Prado, the "Mambo King," receives one of the first gold records
TV game show scandal investigation begins
United Kingdom issues regional stamps (Northern Ireland, Scotland, and Wales)
US performs atmospheric nuclear test at Enewetak Atoll in the Marshall Islands
Verne Gagne defeats Edouard Carpentier in Omaha to become NWA champion
Milwaukee Braves pitcher Lew Burdette no-hits the Philadelphia Phillies 1-0 at County Stadium in Milwaukee
The Beatles make their first public performance at the Indra Club in Hamburg, Germany
The first photograph bounces off a satellite between Cedar Rapids, Iowa, and Richardson, Texas
Peter, Paul & Mary release their first hit, "If I Had a Hammer"
Charles Helu is elected president of Lebanon
South Africa is banned from the Olympic Games because of apartheid policies
The Beatles arrive in San Francisco, California, on their second US visit, and first cross-country tour, encompassing 32 shows in 25 cities over 31 days
USSR launch three Kosmos satellites
Dutch soccer team FC Den Bosch (successor to BVV 1906 and Wilhelmina 1890) is established at 's-Hertogenbosch

Boston Red Sox player Tony Conigliaro is beaned by Angels pitcher Jack Hamilton at Fenway Park; injuries including a fractured cheekbone, dislocated jaw, and eye damage keep him from returning for a year and a half and lead to improvements in batting helmets
Mick Jaggeraccidentally shot while filming "Ned Kelly" in Australia; he survives
Hank Aaron's record of 1,378 extra-base hits surpasses Stan Musial's record
USSR's Luna 24 soft-lands on the Moon
South African anti-apartheid activist Steve Biko is arrested at a roadblock and dies from a police beating on September 12
Two girls are killed by a runaway car outside of Graceland
Memphis, Tennessee, settles with striking police officers and firefighters
USSR performs a nuclear test at the Semipalatinsk Test Site in northeast Kazakhstan
Football running back Herschel Walker of the University of Georgia takes out a Lloyd's of London insurance policy for $1 million
Japanese election law is amended to allow for proportional representation
Longest baseball game at Wrigley Field in Chicago, IL, ends after 22 innings as LA Dodgers beat Cubs 2-1 (game started August 17th)
NYSE tops 100 million shares traded for the first time, setting a new record of 132.69 million shares traded
Hurricane Alicia batters Houston and Galveston, Texas
Samantha Druce, age 12 years and 119 days, becomes the youngest woman to swim the English Channel
Survey of recently discovered Bronze Age Uluburun shipwreck (ca. 1320 B.C.) off the coast of Turkey finds rich cargo of glass, spices, and enough tin and copper for 5,000 swords
USSR performs a nuclear test in Novaya Zemlya, USSR
Triangle Oil Corp's above-ground storage tank in Jacksonville, Florida, spills 2.5 million gallons of oil after lightning ignites a fire
Suisei Launch (Halley's Comet Flyby)
Crockett's Tavern opens in Fort Wilderness
Jim Kelly signs with the NFL's Buffalo Bills for $75 million over 5 years
John Tesh's first appearance on Entertainment Tonight
WYSP 94.1 FM in Philadelphia, Pennsylvania, begins simulcasting the Howard Stern Radio Show from New York City's WXRK
Houston Oiler Earl Campbell retires from the NFL
Ohio healthcare worker Donald Harvey is sentenced to triple life for poisoning 24 patients
Philip Rush of New Zealand sets a record for a triple crossing of the English Channel with a time of 28:21, 10 hours faster than the first man to do it
Strasbourg: Manuela Stellmach, Astrid Strauss, Anke Mohring, and Heike Friedrich swim a women's world record in the 4 x 200 m freestyle (7:55.47)
FDA approves Minoxidil as a hair loss treatment
Largest house (130 rooms) on Long Island sold for $22 million
Republican Convention in New Orleans selects Bush-Quayle ticket
ArenaBowl III takes place at Joe Louis Arena in Detroit: Detroit Drive defeats Pittsburgh Gladiators 39-26, with George LaFrance as MVP
Arturo Barrios of Mexico sets a 10 km record (27:08.23) in Berlin
Bucky Dent replaces Dallas Green as the NY Yankees manager
Leading presidential hopeful Luis Carlos Galán is assassinated near Bogotá in Colombia
Hurricane Bob hits North Carolina with 115 mph winds
Pan American Games close in Havana
Kapellbrücke in Lucerne, Switzerland, is destroyed by fire
15th Commonwealth Games open in Victoria, Canada
5.6-magnitude earthquake in Algeria kills 171
Record 6,654 tap dancers in a single routine at Macy's Tap-O-mania in New York City

A Federal jury finds the US Environmental Protection Agency guilty of discrimination against Dr. Marsha Coleman-Adebayo, under the Civil Rights Act of 1964, later inspiring passage of the No FEAR Act.
Dutch swimmer Pieter van den Hoogenband wins the coveted 100 m freestyle gold medal in 48.17 ahead of Roland Schoeman of South Africa at the Athens Olympics
Japanese swimmer Kosuke Kitajima wraps up the 100/200 m breaststroke double at the Athens Olympics as he wins the 200 m in 2:09.44, an Olympic record

Shot put event at the Athens Olympics is held in Ancient Olympia, site of the ancient Olympic Games; first time women ever compete in the venue; Yumileidi Cumbá of Cuba wins women's gold; American Adam Nelson wins men's gold

US women's 4 × 200 m freestyle relay team of Natalie Coughlin, Carly Piper, Dana Vollmer, and Kaitlin Sandeno smash the long-standing world record set by GDR in 1987 to win gold at the Athens Olympics (7:53.42)
Dennis Rader is sentenced to 175 years in prison for the BTK (bind, torture, kill) serial killings in Sedgwick County, Kansas
Massive power blackout hits the Indonesian island of Java, affecting almost 100 million people
An American 1-2-3 in the 400 m hurdles at the Beijing Olympics; Angelo Taylor wins gold in 47.25 ahead of Kerron Clement and Bershawn Jackson
Belarusian weightlifter Andrei Aramnau breaks three world records in the snatch, clean and jerk, and total on his way to winning the men's 105 kg gold medal at the Beijing Olympics
Emulating the Chinese women, the men win the team table tennis gold medal at the Beijing Olympics without losing a match, winning all 10 singles and all 5 doubles matches
President of Pakistan Pervez Musharaf resigns due to pressure from opposition
Russian pole vaulter Yelena Isinbayeva sets a new world record of 5.05m to win the gold medal at the Beijing Olympics, her 24th world record
The "West Memphis Three" are released from prison after 18 years of imprisonment
Al-Qaeda militants kill 14 people in an attack in Aden, Yemen
NATO airstrikes kill at least 13 militants in Afghanistan
Six people are killed by a bomb blast on a bus in West Bengal, India

Great Britain's Alistair Brownlee records a time of 1:45:01 to retain his Olympic men's triathlon title at the Rio de Janeiro Games, finishing 0:06 ahead of second-place finisher, his brother Jonathan Brownlee
Ukrainian canoeist Yuriy Cheban sets an Olympic record of 39.279 to win the men's C-1 200 m gold at the Rio de Janeiro Olympics; retains the title he won in London 2012
Archaeologists confirm the first-ever cheese found from Ancient Egypt, 3200 years old, in the tomb of Ptahmes, mayor of Memphis
Australian mare Winx wins her Australasian record 26th consecutive race, the renamed Winx Stakes at Royal Randwick in Sydney, achieving her 19th Group One win for thoroughbreds
1.7 million people participate in a peaceful pro-democracy protest in Hong Kong, representing a quarter of the population
Iceland holds a formal ceremony for the first glacier lost to climate change at the site of the Okjökull Glacier
Mali's President Ibrahim Boubacar Keïta resigns amid a military coup condemned by the UN Security Council
A huge megalithic complex of more than 500 standing stones is announced as discovered at La Torre-La Janera, Huelva, in southern Spain
New method to break down "forever chemicals" PFAS compounds, responsible for cancers, low birth weights, and lowered immunity, published by scientists in the journal "Science"
Canadian province of British Columbia declares a state of emergency as two major wildfires combine near Adams Lake, forcing the evacuation of 30,000 people
English neonatal nurse Lucy Letby is found guilty of the murder of seven babies and the attempted murder of six more between June 2015 and June 2016 at a Manchester court
Paetongtarn Shinawatra is sworn in as Thailand's youngest-ever Prime Minister at 37 years old
Leaders from across Europe join Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelenskyy at a hastily arranged summit meeting with US President Trump at the White House
Marius Borg Høiby, son of the Norwegian Crown Princess, is charged with 32 offenses, including rape and domestic violence
New York Yankees hit 9 home runs during 13-3 win over the Rays at George M. Steinbrenner Field in Tampa Bay, Florida, becoming first franchise in MLB history to record two games with nine homers
Spanish KingPhilip IIjoins Council of Trent
Jean Racine's dramatic tragedy "Iphigénie" premieres in Versailles
Russian TsarPeter the Greatarrives in Zaandam
United States Exploring Expedition headed byCharles Wilkesdeparts for the Pacific Ocean and Antarctica
General Lee's adjutant, Major J.E..B. Stuart's hat is captured
Mayor of Tokyo Yukio Ozaki presents Washington, D.C. with 2,000 cherry trees, which PresidentWilliam Howard Taftdecides to plant near the Potomac River
The Queen's Hospital opens to provide pioneering plastic surgery for WWI soldiers, led byHarold Gilliesin Sidcup, England
Lou Gehrigis hitless in Detroit in his 1,000th consecutive game
Don Bradman(244) andBill Ponsford(205 not out) put on 451 in a world record partnership for Australia on Day 1 of the 5th cricket Test win against England at The Oval
Franklin D. Rooseveltdedicates the Thousand Islands Bridge connecting the US and Canada
Final convoy of Jews from Salonika, Greece, arrives atAuschwitz
Giants' future Baseball Hall of Fame pitcherCarl Hubbellwins his 253rd and final game as NY beats the Pittsburgh Pirates 3-2 at the Polo Grounds in NYC
Chartres is freed by US 3rd Army forces during WWII led by GeneralGeorge S. Patton
Sukarnois elected as the first President of Indonesia by the Preparatory Committee for Indonesian Independence (PPKI)
Elvis Presley's "Hound Dog" and "Don't Be Cruel" reach #1 on the charts, staying for 11 weeks (a record for a single release)
Fidel Castromakes a speech on Cuban pirate radio Rebelde
Floyd PattersonTKOs Roy Harris in the 13th round for the heavyweight boxing title at Wrigley Field in Los Angeles, California
Novel "Lolita" byVladimir Nabokovis published
Branch Rickeyresigns as MLB Pittsburgh Pirates' CEO to become President of the new baseball Continental League
James Meredithbecomes the first Black graduate from the University of Mississippi with a degree in political science
Hank Aaronloses a home run because he hits it out of the batter's box
Orioles'Brooks Robinsonhits into his third career triple play, tying George Sisler, Deacon McGuire, and Joe Start

The Rolling Stonesrelease the music single "We Love You," written byMick JaggerandKeith Richardsas a message of thanks to fans for their support after the duo's recent drug arrests; the recording features backing vocals byJohn LennonandPaul McCartney

New Zealand Prime MinisterKeith Holyoakeannounces in Parliament that New Zealand’s combat force would be withdrawn from Vietnam before the end of the year, coinciding with a similar announcement by the Australian government
Police fine Paul andLinda McCartney800 Swedish kronor in Sweden for cannabis possession
Drummer Gene Krupa plays for the final time, appearing with theBenny GoodmanQuartet in Saratoga Springs, New York

Korean axe murder incident: two US soldiers tasked with cutting down a poplar tree blocking the view of UN observers are killed by North Koreans, claiming it was planted byKim Il-Sungin the Korean Demilitarized Zone
Dodgers pitcherDon Suttonthrows his NL record-tying fifth one-hitter
Ayatollah Khomeiniof Iran demands a Jihad against Kurdish separatists
KC Royals'George Brett's batting average reaches .400
Jerry Lewisappears on "Donahue" to defend telethons
MLB Philadelphia Phillies'Pete Rosesets a record with his 13,941st plate appearance
KC Royals defeat NY Yankees 5-4, completing the "pine-tar" game in 12 minutes; Hal McRae strikes out andDan Quisenberryretires Yankees in order
American rock bandBon Jovireleases their third album "Slippery When Wet" (Billboard's top-selling album of 1987)
William H. Webster(81), former head of the FBI and later CIA director, becomes chairman of the Homeland Security Advisory Council, serving under three presidential administrations until 2020
Jamaica'sUsain Boltwins the gold medal in the men's 200 m for the third successive Summer Olympics, recording a time of 19.78 seconds in Rio de Janeiro
New Zealand's 2017 America's Cup-winning helmsmanPeter Burlingteams with Blair Tuke to win the gold medal in the 49er class sailing at the Rio de Janeiro Olympics
White House chief strategist Stephen Bannon is fired by US PresidentDonald Trump

Imran Khanbecomes the 22nd Prime Minister of Pakistan after gaining the support of independent members following his party, the Pakistan Tehreek-e-Insaf (PTI), winning the most votes but not a majority in the general election
California GovernorGavin Newsomdeclares a state of emergency as 27 fires burn across the state amid a continuing heat wave
Joe Bidenis formally nominated as the Democratic Party's presidential candidate during the second night of their first-ever virtual convention
MLB Los Angeles Angels pitcherShohei Ohtanibecomes the fastest player in team history to reach 40 home runs in a season and also pitches 8 innings in a 3-1 win at Detroit

Ukrainian President VolodymyrZelenskyy, Turkish leader Recep TayyipErdogan, and UN Secretary-GeneralAntónio Guterreswarn of "another Chernobyl" due to fighting near the Zaporizhzhia nuclear plant at a summit in Lviv
Competition is announced to design the dome of Florence Cathedral, with main competitors Lorenzo Ghiberti and Filippo Brunelleschi (supported byCosimo de' Medici)
Chris Watson resigns as Prime Minister of Australia and is succeeded by George Reid

State Representative Harry T. Burn (24) casts the deciding vote in Tennessee's and thus America's ratification of the 19th Amendment to the Constitution, allowing women's suffrage after reading a letter from his mother
Battle of Britain: Air battle known as "The Hardest Day" occurs; the Luftwaffe loses approximately 69 aircraft and the RAF 68 in one of the largest ever air battles
Civilian researchers led byPaul Allenrediscover USS Indianapolis 18,000 feet below the Pacific surface 72 years after it is sunk by Japanese torpedoes
Indian actressPriyanka Chopraand American singer Nick Jonas confirm their engagement with a party in Mumbai, India
Ashikaga Takauji, Japanese founder and 1st shōgun of the Ashikaga shogunate (1338-58), born in Ashikaga, Japan (d. 1358)
Marko Marulic/Marulus/Splichanin/Pechinich, Croatian poet, born in Split, Croatia (d. 1524)
Francesco da Milano, Italian lutenist and composer, also known as 'Il divino', born in Monza (d. 1543)
Charlotte Flandrina of Nassau, French countess, abbess and daughter of William I of Orange, born in Antwerp (d. 1640)
Virginia Dare, 1st English child born in the New World, born on Roanoke Island, North Carolina (fate unknown)[1]
Jean Bolland, Flemish Jesuit writer and historian (Acta Sanctorum), born in Tirlemont, Netherlands (d. 1665)
Henry Hammond, English churchman, born in Chertsey, Surrey, England (d. 1660)
Maria Anna of Spain, Holy Roman Empire Empress and Queen of Hungary, born in El Escorial, San Lorenzo de El Escorial, Spain (d. 1646)
Ludwika Maria Gonzaga, French princess and queen of Poland, born in Nevers, France (d. 1667)
Ferdinando Galli Bibiena, Italian architect and designer, born in Bologna, Italy (d. 1743)
Brook Taylor, English mathematician, discoverer of Taylor's Theorem, born in Edmonton, Middlesex, England (d. 1731)
Louis Henri, Duc de Bourbon, Prime Minister of France, born in Palace of Versailles, France (d. 1740)
Laurence Shirley, 4th Earl Ferrers, last English peer to be hanged for murdering his steward, born in England (d. 1760)
Vaclav Praupner, Bohemian violinist, organist, choirmaster, and composer (Circe), born in Litoměřice, Kingdom of Bohemia (d. 1807)
François, Marquis de Chasseloup-Laubat, French general and military engineer, born in Saint-Sornin, France (d. 1833)
Alexandre Stievenard, French composer and musician, born in Cambrai, France (d. 1855)
Nathan Clifford, American statesman, diplomat, and Justice of the U.S. Supreme Court, born in Rumney, New Hampshire, (d. 1881)
Josef Danhauser, Austrian painter (Mutterliebe), born in Vienna, Austria (d. 1845)
Charles Francis Adams Sr., American writer and diplomat, US Minister to the UK, son of President John Quincy Adams, born in Boston, Massachusetts (d. 1886)
Benjamin Alvord, American scientist and Brigadier General (Union Army), born in Vancouver, Washington (d. 1884)
William Farquhar Barry, American Brevet Major General (Union Army), born in New York City (d. 1879)
Grand Duchess Maria Nikolaevna, Duchess of Leuchtenberg, born in Peterhof Palace, Russia (d. 1876)
Willem Jan Frans Nuyens, Dutch physician and Roman Catholic historian, born in Avenhorn, Netherlands (d. 1894)
Marshall Field, American entrepreneur and the founder of Marshall Field and Company, born in Conway, Massachusetts (d. 1906)
Robert Williams Buchanan, Scottish, poet, dramatist and novelist, born in Caverswall, Staffordshire, England. (d. 1901)

William Halford, American naval officer and Medal of Honor recipient who survived 31 days at sea in a small boat to seek help for the shipwrecked USS Saginaw, born in Gloucestershire, England (d. 1919)
Benjamin Godard, French violinist, orchestral and opera composer (Symphonie légendaire; Jocelyn), born in Paris, France (d. 1895)
Alfred Wallis, English artist and mariner, born in Devonport, Devon, England (d. 1942)
Charles H. Gabriel, American gospel music composer ("Will the Circle Be Unbroken", "I Stand Amazed"), born in Wilton, Iowa (d. 1932)
Jan Karol Gall, Polish conductor an composer of mostly vocal pieces (Mizerna, cicha), born in Warsaw, Congress Poland (d. 1912)
Libert H. Boeynaems, Belgian Catholic prelate and the fourth vicar apostolic of the Vicariate Apostolic of the Hawaiian Islands, born in Antwerp, Belgium (d. 1926)
Hugh Bromley-Davenport, England cricketer, born in Chelford, Cheshire, England (d. 1954)
Leo Slezak, Moravian dramatic tenor and actor (Othello; Zirkus Saran; Husaren heraus), born in Mährisch-Schönberg, Austria-Hungary (now Šumperk, Czech Republic) (d. 1946)
Otto Harbach [Hauerbach], American librettist, lyricist ("Smoke Gets in Your Eyes", "I Won't Dance"), and mentor to Oscar Hammerstein II, born in New York City (d. 1963)
Johan E. Elias, Dutch historian (Amsterdam's regency), born in Amsterdam (d. 1960)
Fritz Brun, Swiss pianist, composer and conductor (Bernischen Musikgesellschaft, 1906-41), born in Lucerne, Switzerland (d. 1959)
Aleksandr Rodzyanko, Russian lieutenant-general and corps commander of the White Army during the Russian Civil War, born in Russia (d. 1970)
Hermann Zilcher, German pianist and composer (Dr Eisenbart; Die Liebesmesse), born in Frankfurt am Main, Germany (d. 1948)
Marcel Samuel-Rousseau, French organist and opera composer (Le Bon Roi Dagobert), born in Paris (d. 1955)
Nettie Palmer, Australian poet and essayist (Henry Handel Richardson: A Study), born in Bendigo, Victoria, Australia (d. 1964)
Nico J. Polak, Dutch economist, born in Rotterdam, Netherlands (d. 1948)

Maurice Podoloff, American attorney, professional sports administrator (AHL President, 1943-47; NBA President, 1946-63), and namesake of NBA regular season MVP Award, born in 
Yelisavetgrad, Russian Empire (now Ukraine) (d. 1985) [birth details unconfirmed]
Walther Funk, German economist and Nazi politician, born in Danzkehmen, Kingdom of Prussia, German Empire (d. 1960)

Burleigh Grimes, American Baseball HOF pitcher (World Series 1931 St. Louis Cardinals; NL wins leader 1921, 28; NL strikeout leader 1921 Brooklyn Robins, Pittsburgh Pirates), born in Emerald, Wisconsin (d. 1985)
Ernest MacMillan, Canadian organist, conductor and composer, born in Etobicoke, Ontario (d. 1973)
Alan Mowbray, British actor (Dante, Colonel Flack), born in London, England (d. 1969)
Jack Pickford, Canadian-American actor (The Little Shepherd of Kingdom Come), born in Toronto, Ontario (d. 1933)
Glenn Albert Black, American archaeologist, born in Indianapolis, Indiana (d. 1964)
Walter O'Keefe, American songwriter, radio and TV host (Mayor of Hollywood), born in Hartford, Connecticut (d. 1983)
Adamson-Eric, Estonian painter, born in Tartu, Estonia (d. 1968)
Julius Kalaš, Czech composer (Nepokořenĺ -The Proud Ones), born in Prague, Kingdom of Bohemia (d. 1967)
Åke Olof Sebastian Uddén, Swedish violist, composer and educator (Royal College Of Music, 1934-70), born in Lossa, Swsden (d. 1987)
Lucienne Boyer, French singer ("Parlez-moi d'amour"), born in Paris (d. 1983)
W. Sterling Cole, American politician (Rep-R-NY, Director International Atomic Energy Agency), born in Painted Post, New York (d. 1987)
[Francis] Max Factor, American CEO of Max Factor Cosmetics, born in St. Louis, Missouri (d. 1996)
Peter Paul Kreuder, German-Austrian pianist and film score composer, born in Aachen, Germany (d. 1981)
Andre Van Gyseghem, English actor (Search for the Nile), born in Eltham, Kent, England (d. 1979)
Curtis Jones, American blues pianist and songwriter ("Lonesome Bedroom Blues", "Highway 51 Blues"), born in Naples, Texas (d. 1971)
Cecilia Grace Hunt Reeves Gillie, Executive (BBC), born in Sheppey, Kent, England (d. 1996)
Enoch Light, American orchestra leader (Gulf Road Show with Bob Smith), born in Canton, Ohio (d. 1978)
Howard Swanson, American composer known for his settings of poems of Langston Hughes, born in Atlanta, Georgia (d. 1978)
Otto Mortensen, Danish composer and musicologist, born in Copenhagen, Denmark (d. 1986)
Armijn Pane, Indonesian writer (Belenggu), born in North Sumatra, Indonesia (d. 1970)
Bill Merritt, New Zealand cricket spin bowler (6 Tests, 12 wickets; Canterbury, Northamstonshire CCC), born in Christchurch, New Zealand (d. 1977)
Edgar Faure, Prime Minister of France (1952, 52-56) and writer, born in Béziers, France (d. 1988)
Milan Ristić, Serbian composer, born in Belgrade, Serbia (d. 1982)
Miliza Korjus, Polish actress (Great Waltz), born in Warsaw, Poland (d. 1980)
Herman Berlinski, Polish-American organist, composer and choral conductor (The Tetragrammaton), born in Leipzig, Germany (d. 2001)[1]
Elsa Morante, Italian writer, born in Rome (d. 1985)
Max Lanier, American baseball pitcher (MLB All Star 1943-44; World Series 1942, 44; St. Louis Cardinals), born in Denton, North Carolina (d. 2007)
Moura Lympany [Mary Johnstone], British concert pianist (OBE-1979), born in Saltash England (d. 2009)
Casper Weinberger, American politician and businessman (US Secretary of Defense under Reagan 1981-87), born in San Francisco, California (d. 2006)
(Gilbert) "Cisco" Houston, American folk singer and songwriter, born in Wilmington, Delaware (d. 1961)
[Walter J] Wally Hickel, American politician (Governor-R-Alaska 1966-69, US Secretary of Interior 1969-71), born in Ellinwood, Kansas (d. 2010)
Bob Kennedy, American baseball utility (World Series 1948 Cleveland Indians) and manager (Chicago Cubs, Oakland A's), born in Chicago, Illinois (d. 2005)
Godfrey Evans, English cricket wicket-keeper (91 Tests; 219 dismissals; Kent CCC), born in Finchley, England (d. 1999)
Joe Caroff, American graphic designer responsible for the James Bond 007 logo, born in Roselle, New Jersey (d. 2025)[1]
Zdzisław Żygulski Jr., Polish art historian, born in Borysław, Poland (d. 2015)
Alain Robbe-Grillet, French novelist (Voyeur) and film  maker, born in Brest, France (d. 2008)
Eric William Hunter Christie, British lawyer and advocate for the Falkland Islands, born in Downe, England Kent (d. 1997)
Jenni Irani, Indian cricketer, born in Karachi (d. 1982)
Sadu Shinde, Indian cricket spin bowler (7 Tests, 12 wickets, BB 6/91; Maharashtra, Bombay, Baroda), born in Mumbai, India (d. 1955)
Brian Aldiss, British sci-fi author (Super-Toys Last All Summer Long, Helliconia trilogy), born in East Dereham, Norfolk (d. 2017)
Pierre Grondin, Canadian pioneering cardiac surgeon, performed 1st heart transplant in Canada, born in Montreal, Quebec (d. 2006)
Sonny Til [Earlington Carl Tilghman], American doo-wop singer (The Orioles - "It's Too Soon to Know", "Crying in the Chapel"), born in Baltimore, Maryland (d. 1981)
Rosalynn Carter (née Smith), American activist and First Lady (1977-81), born in Plains, Georgia (d. 2023)[1]
Marge Schott, American MLB owner (Cincinnati Reds), born in Cincinnati, Ohio (d. 2004)
Hugues Aufray, French songwriter, singer, guitarist, and Bob Dylan translator, born in Neuilly-sur-Seine, France
Carl Barger, American baseball executive (President of the Pittsburgh Pirates, Florida Marlins), born in Lewistown, Pennsylvania (d. 1992)
Gene Bartow, American College Basketball Hall of Fame coach (US National Team 1974; Valparaiso, Memphis State, Illinois, UCLA), born in Browning, Missouri (d. 2012)
Liviu Librescu, Romanian-American professor (sacificed his life to save students during Virginia Tech massacre), born in Ploiești, Romania (d. 2007)
